preamble.summary: Proverbs 11 continues the Solomonic collection of antithetic proverbs, pairing righteous and wicked conduct in nearly every verse. The chapter's dominant concern is the social consequences of character: how honesty or dishonesty, generosity or greed, humility or arrogance shape a person's standing in the community and determine their ultimate fate.
preamble.remarkable: This chapter exhibits an almost obsessive interest in economic justice. Dishonest scales open the chapter, and the theme of wealth — how it is gained, how it is used, whether it endures — runs through at least a third of the verses. The collection insists that generosity produces abundance while hoarding produces scarcity, an economic paradox that defies conventional logic. The repeated claim that righteousness delivers from death is not a promise of immortality but a statement about the structural reliability of moral order: those who live rightly avoid the traps that destroy the wicked.
preamble.friction: The clean antithesis between righteous and wicked outcomes in these proverbs represents idealized wisdom. The sages knew that life often fails to match these tidy pairings — Job and Ecclesiastes exist as canonical correctives. These proverbs function as general observations about how the moral order tends to work, not as ironclad guarantees. The modern reader must hold them in tension with the rest of the wisdom tradition.
preamble.connections: The dishonest-scales imagery in verse 1 echoes Leviticus 19:35-36 and Deuteronomy 25:13-16, where fraudulent weights are called an abomination to the LORD. The generosity paradox in verses 24-25 anticipates the teaching of Jesus in Luke 6:38. The 'gold ring in a pig's snout' metaphor of verse 22 is unique in the Hebrew Bible — a vivid image found nowhere else in Scripture.

verses.text_hebrew: מֹאזְנֵ֣י מִ֭רְמָה תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְהוָ֑ה וְאֶ֖בֶן שְׁלֵמָ֣ה רְצוֹנֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: A false balance is abomination to the LORD: but a just weight is his delight.
verses.rendering:
Dishonest scales are detestable to the LORD,
but an accurate weight is His delight.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verse opens with mo'azne mirmah ('scales of deceit') — the commercial fraud of rigging a balance to cheat buyers or sellers. This is not merely unethical business practice; it is to'avat Yahweh, 'an abomination to the LORD,' the strongest term of divine revulsion in Hebrew. The counterpart is even shelemah ('a full, complete, honest stone-weight'), which earns God's ratson ('delight, favor, acceptance'). The marketplace becomes a theater of covenant loyalty or betrayal. God's concern extends to the grain merchant's weights because economic honesty reflects the character of the covenant community.
translator_notes: The word even ('stone') refers to the stone weights used on balance scales in ancient commerce. An even shelemah is a weight that has not been shaved down or hollowed out to defraud.

The pairing of to'evah ('abomination') and ratson ('delight') establishes the emotional poles of God's response to human conduct. What disgusts God and what pleases God — the chapter will elaborate both categories.

key_terms.semantic_range: abomination, something detestable, loathsome act; what provokes God's revulsion
key_terms.note: to'evah marks conduct that is not merely wrong but deeply offensive to God's nature. It appears frequently in Proverbs for dishonesty, pride, and injustice — matters the sages considered violations of the created moral order.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּֽא־זָ֭דוֹן וַיָּבֹ֣א קָל֑וֹן וְֽאֶת־צְנוּעִ֥ים חׇכְמָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: When pride cometh, then cometh shame: but with the lowly is wisdom.
verses.rendering:
When arrogance arrives, disgrace follows,
but wisdom stays with the humble.
translator_notes: The Hebrew is strikingly compressed: ba zadon va-yavo qalon — 'comes arrogance, and comes disgrace.' The two arrivals are presented as virtually simultaneous, as though arrogance carries disgrace inside it like cargo.
The tsenu'im ('humble, modest') are not self-deprecating people but those who have an accurate assessment of their own position. Wisdom resides with them because they remain teachable.

key_terms.semantic_range: modest, humble, unassuming; those who do not overestimate themselves
key_terms.note: This word appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its rarity gives it force — the sages coined or preserved a rare term to name the opposite of arrogance.

verses.text_hebrew: תֻּמַּ֣ת יְשָׁרִ֣ים תַּנְחֵ֑ם וְסֶ֖לֶף בּוֹגְדִ֣ים יְשָׁדֵּֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The integrity of the upright shall guide them: but the perverseness of transgressors shall destroy them.
verses.rendering:
The integrity of the upright guides them,
but the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them.
translator_notes: Tummah ('integrity, completeness, wholeness') comes from the same root as tam ('blameless, complete'). It describes a person whose inner character and outward conduct are aligned — no hidden agenda, no double life.
Selef ('crookedness, perverseness') suggests something twisted or distorted. The treacherous are destroyed not by external enemies but by their own warped character.

verses.text_hebrew: לֹא־יוֹעִ֣יל ה֭וֹן בְּי֣וֹם עֶבְרָ֑ה וּ֝צְדָקָ֗ה תַּצִּ֥יל מִמָּֽוֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: Riches profit not in the day of wrath: but righteousness delivereth from death.
verses.rendering:
Wealth is useless on the day of fury,
but righteousness rescues from death.
verses.expanded_rendering: The yom evrah ('day of fury, day of wrath') is the moment when accumulated consequences crash down — whether divine judgment, social collapse, or personal catastrophe. On that day, hon ('wealth, possessions') has no purchasing power. Money cannot bribe death or buy off calamity. Only tsedaqah ('righteousness') has the power to deliver (tatsil, from natsal, 'to snatch away, to rescue'). The verse does not explain the mechanism; it simply asserts that right living creates a kind of protection that wealth cannot replicate.
translator_notes: The 'day of wrath' is not necessarily eschatological. In wisdom literature, it refers to any moment of crisis when a person's true resources are tested. Wealth fails the test; character passes it.

key_terms.semantic_range: righteousness, justice, right conduct, covenant faithfulness; conformity to God's moral order
key_terms.note: tsedaqah in Proverbs is both ethical character and its social expression — doing what is right toward God and neighbor. It is the quality that endures when every external advantage has been stripped away.

verses.text_hebrew: צִדְקַ֣ת תָּ֭מִים תְּיַשֵּׁ֣ר דַּרְכּ֑וֹ וּ֝בְרִשְׁעָת֗וֹ יִפֹּ֥ל רָשָֽׁע׃
verses.text_kjv: The righteousness of the perfect shall direct his way: but the wicked shall fall by his own wickedness.
verses.rendering:
The righteousness of the blameless makes his path straight,
but the wicked falls by his own wickedness.
translator_notes: The tamim ('blameless, complete') is not sinless but wholehearted — a person of undivided loyalty. His righteousness functions as a road-straightener, removing obstacles and keeping the way clear.
The reflexive logic is crucial: the wicked falls be-rish'ato ('by his own wickedness'). No external agent is needed. Wickedness is self-destroying.

verses.text_hebrew: צִדְקַ֣ת יְ֭שָׁרִים תַּצִּילֵ֑ם וּ֝בְהַוַּ֗ת בֹּגְדִ֥ים יִלָּכֵֽדוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The righteousness of the upright shall deliver them: but transgressors shall be taken in their own naughtiness.
verses.rendering:
The righteousness of the upright rescues them,
but the treacherous are trapped by their own craving.
translator_notes: Havvat ('craving, desire, greed, destructive appetite') is from havvah, which carries overtones of ruin and disaster. The treacherous are not trapped by external snares but by their own disordered desires.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּמ֤וֹת אָדָ֣ם רָ֭שָׁע תֹּאבַ֣ד תִּקְוָ֑ה וְתוֹחֶ֖לֶת אוֹנִ֣ים אָבָֽדָה׃
verses.text_kjv: When a wicked man dieth, his expectation shall perish: and the hope of unjust men perisheth.
verses.rendering:
When a wicked person dies, his hope perishes,
and the expectation of the powerful comes to nothing.
translator_notes: The onim ('powerful, vigorous') in the parallel line may refer to those who trust in their own strength or resources. Death neutralizes every advantage. The verse is a memento mori — a reminder that death exposes the bankruptcy of a life built on wickedness or raw power.

verses.text_hebrew: צַ֭דִּיק מִצָּרָ֣ה נֶחֱלָ֑ץ וַיָּבֹ֖א רָשָׁ֣ע תַּחְתָּֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: The righteous is delivered out of trouble, and the wicked cometh in his stead.
verses.rendering:
The righteous is rescued from distress,
and the wicked steps into his place.
translator_notes: The image is of a swap: the righteous escapes the trap, and the wicked walks into the same trap. The Hebrew tachatav ('in his place, instead of him') suggests a direct substitution — the wicked inherits the trouble that was meant for the righteous.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭פֶה חָנֵ֣ף יַשְׁחִ֣ת רֵעֵ֑הוּ וּ֝בְדַ֗עַת צַדִּיקִ֥ים יֵחָלֵֽצוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: An hypocrite with his mouth destroyeth his neighbour: but through knowledge shall the just be delivered.
verses.rendering:
With his mouth a godless person destroys his neighbor,
but through knowledge the righteous are rescued.
translator_notes: Chanef ('godless, profane, hypocritical') describes someone whose speech is disconnected from truth or loyalty. The mouth becomes a weapon of destruction against the re'a ('neighbor, companion, fellow'). Knowledge — da'at, discernment that sees through deception — is the righteous person's defense.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּט֣וּב צַ֭דִּיקִים תַּעֲלֹ֣ץ קִרְיָ֑ה וּבַאֲבֹ֖ד רְשָׁעִ֣ים רִנָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: When it goeth well with the righteous, the city rejoiceth: and when the wicked perish, there is shouting.
verses.rendering:
When the righteous prosper, the city celebrates;
when the wicked perish, there is joyful shouting.
translator_notes: The qiryah ('city, town') is the social unit that benefits or suffers from the character of its members. Individual righteousness has communal consequences — the city itself rejoices. The double celebration (for righteous flourishing and wicked removal) underscores how deeply the community's welfare depends on moral leadership.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּבִרְכַּ֣ת יְ֭שָׁרִים תָּר֣וּם קָ֑רֶת וּבְפִ֥י רְ֝שָׁעִ֗ים תֵּהָרֵֽס׃
verses.text_kjv: By the blessing of the upright the city is exalted: but it is overthrown by the mouth of the wicked.
verses.rendering:
By the blessing of the upright a city is lifted up,
but by the mouth of the wicked it is torn down.
translator_notes: Birkat ('blessing of') suggests both the prayers and the beneficial influence of the upright. Their presence elevates the entire community. The wicked's mouth — their counsel, slander, and deceitful speech — has the opposite effect, pulling the social fabric apart.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּז־לְרֵעֵ֥הוּ חֲסַר־לֵ֑ב וְאִ֖ישׁ תְּבוּנ֣וֹת יַחֲרִֽישׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is void of wisdom despiseth his neighbour: but a man of understanding holdeth his peace.
verses.rendering:
Whoever despises his neighbor lacks sense,
but a person of understanding keeps silent.
translator_notes: Chasar lev ('lacking heart') means lacking judgment or intelligence — the 'heart' in Hebrew is the seat of thought, not emotion. Contempt for a neighbor reveals intellectual emptiness. The person of understanding (tevunot, 'discernments') knows when silence is the wiser course.

verses.text_hebrew: הוֹלֵ֣ךְ רָ֭כִיל מְגַלֶּה־סּ֑וֹד וְנֶאֱמַן־ר֝֗וּחַ מְכַסֶּ֥ה דָבָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: A talebearer revealeth secrets: but he that is of a faithful spirit concealeth the matter.
verses.rendering:
A gossip reveals secrets,
but a trustworthy person conceals a matter.
translator_notes: Rakhil ('gossip, slanderer, talebearer') is someone who trades in other people's confidences. The ne'eman ruach ('faithful of spirit, trustworthy in character') is the opposite — a person who can be trusted with sensitive information because their loyalty runs deeper than the thrill of sharing secrets.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful, reliable, trustworthy, confirmed, established; from aman ('to be firm, to support')
key_terms.note: ne'eman shares its root with emunah ('faithfulness') and amen. A ne'eman ruach is a person whose inner character is solid and dependable — they can bear weight without breaking.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאֵ֣ין תַּ֭חְבֻּלוֹת יִפׇּל־עָ֑ם וּ֝תְשׁוּעָ֗ה בְּרֹ֣ב יוֹעֵֽץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Where no counsel is, the people fall: but in the multitude of counsellors there is safety.
verses.rendering:
Without guidance a people falls,
but safety comes through many advisors.
translator_notes: Tachbulot ('guidance, direction, steering') comes from a nautical metaphor — the word is related to the ropes used to steer a ship. A nation without skilled navigation will capsize. The teshu'ah ('deliverance, salvation, safety') comes through rov yo'ets ('abundance of counselors') — the wisdom of collective deliberation.

verses.text_hebrew: רַע־יֵ֭רוֹעַ כִּי־עָ֣רַב זָ֑ר וְשֹׂנֵ֖א תֹקְעִ֣ים בּוֹטֵֽחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that is surety for a stranger shall smart for it: and he that hateth suretiship is sure.
verses.rendering:
Whoever guarantees a loan for a stranger will suffer for it,
but the one who refuses to pledge is secure.
translator_notes: The arav ('to pledge, to become surety') involves taking on financial liability for another person's debt. The sages consistently warn against this practice — not because generosity is wrong, but because pledging for a stranger (zar) puts one's household at risk for someone whose reliability is unknown.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵֽשֶׁת־חֵ֭ן תִּתְמֹ֣ךְ כָּב֑וֹד וְ֝עָרִיצִ֗ים יִתְמְכוּ־עֹֽשֶׁר׃
verses.text_kjv: A gracious woman retaineth honour: and strong men retain riches.
verses.rendering:
A gracious woman holds on to honor,
and ruthless men hold on to wealth.
translator_notes: The contrast is subtle: the eshet chen ('woman of grace, gracious woman') grasps kavod ('honor, dignity, weight'), while the aritsim ('ruthless, violent, tyrannical') grasp only osher ('riches'). The implication is that honor outranks wealth, and that the means of acquisition matter — grace versus ruthlessness.

verses.text_hebrew: גֹּמֵ֣ל נַ֭פְשׁוֹ אִ֣ישׁ חָ֑סֶד וְעֹכֵ֥ר שְׁ֝אֵר֗וֹ אַכְזָרִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: The merciful man doeth good to his own soul: but he that is cruel troubleth his own flesh.
verses.rendering:
A person of faithful love benefits himself,
but a cruel person brings trouble on his own body.
translator_notes: The ish chesed ('person of faithful love') is someone whose habitual posture toward others is loyal kindness. The remarkable claim is that this benefits his own nefesh ('soul, self, life') — kindness is not self-sacrifice but self-nourishment. The akhzari ('cruel, merciless') person, by contrast, oker she'ero ('troubles his own flesh') — cruelty consumes the one who practices it.

key_terms.semantic_range: faithful love, loyal kindness, covenant devotion, steadfast mercy; God's defining relational attribute
key_terms.note: chesed is the gravity of covenant relationship — the pull that keeps partners bound to each other through loyalty, generosity, and persistent kindness. Here it describes a human quality that mirrors God's own character.

verses.text_hebrew: רָשָׁ֗ע עֹשֶׂ֥ה פְעֻלַּת־שָׁ֑קֶר וְזֹרֵ֥עַ צְ֝דָקָ֗ה שֶׂ֣כֶר אֱמֶֽת׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked worketh a deceitful work: but to him that soweth righteousness shall be a sure reward.
verses.rendering:
The wicked earns deceptive wages,
but the one who sows righteousness reaps a genuine reward.
translator_notes: The agricultural metaphor — sowing and reaping — frames moral conduct as an investment. The wicked's pe'ullat shaqer ('wages of falsehood') look profitable but prove hollow. The one who sows tsedaqah ('righteousness') receives sekher emet ('a reward of truth, a genuine reward') — returns that are real and lasting.

verses.text_hebrew: כֵּן־צְדָקָ֥ה לְחַיִּ֑ים וּמְרַדֵּ֖ף רָעָ֣ה לְמוֹתֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: As righteousness tendeth to life: so he that pursueth evil pursueth it to his own death.
verses.rendering:
Genuine righteousness leads to life,
but whoever chases evil chases his own death.
translator_notes: The verb meradef ('pursuing, chasing') gives the wicked an active, aggressive posture — they are not passively drifting toward evil but running after it. The irony is that what they are actually running toward is their own death.

verses.text_hebrew: תּוֹעֲבַ֣ת יְ֭הוָה עִקְּשֵׁי־לֵ֑ב וּ֝רְצוֹנ֗וֹ תְּמִ֣ימֵי דָֽרֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: They that are of a froward heart are abomination to the LORD: but such as are upright in their way are his delight.
verses.rendering:
The twisted in heart are detestable to the LORD,
but those whose way is blameless are His delight.
translator_notes: Iqqeshe lev ('twisted of heart, crooked-hearted') describes people whose inner orientation is warped — they cannot think straight because their core is bent. The temime derekh ('blameless of way, those whose path is whole') are not perfect but integrated — their conduct matches their convictions.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֣ד לְ֭יָד לֹא־יִנָּ֣קֶה רָּ֑ע וְזֶ֖רַע צַדִּיקִ֣ים נִמְלָֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: Though hand join in hand, the wicked shall not be unpunished: but the seed of the righteous shall be delivered.
verses.rendering:
Be assured: the wicked will not go unpunished,
but the offspring of the righteous will escape.
translator_notes: The idiom yad le-yad ('hand to hand') likely means 'you can count on this' or 'I guarantee it' — a handshake of certainty. The zera tsaddiqim ('seed of the righteous, offspring of the righteous') extends the promise across generations: the consequences of righteous living benefit one's children.

verses.text_hebrew: נֶ֣זֶם זָ֭הָב בְּאַ֣ף חֲזִ֑יר אִשָּׁ֥ה יָ֝פָ֗ה וְסָ֣רַת טָֽעַם׃
verses.text_kjv: As a jewel of gold in a swine's snout, so is a fair woman which is without discretion.
verses.rendering:
A gold ring in a pig's snout —
that is a beautiful woman who abandons good sense.
translator_notes: This is one of the most vivid images in Proverbs. The nezem zahav ('gold ring') is a valuable nose ring, a mark of beauty and status. Placing it in the af chazir ('snout of a pig') creates a jarring absurdity — the precious ornament is wasted on an animal that will drag it through mud. A woman who is yafah ('beautiful') but sarat ta'am ('turned aside from taste/sense, lacking discretion') represents the same mismatch between external form and internal substance.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּאֲוַ֣ת צַדִּיקִ֣ים אַךְ־ט֑וֹב תִּקְוַ֖ת רְשָׁעִ֣ים עֶבְרָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The desire of the righteous is only good: but the expectation of the wicked is wrath.
verses.rendering:
The desire of the righteous leads only to good;
the hope of the wicked leads to fury.
translator_notes: The ta'avah ('desire, longing, appetite') of the righteous is directed toward tov ('good') — their wants align with what is genuinely beneficial. The wicked's tiqvah ('hope, expectation') terminates in evrah ('fury, wrath, overflow') — what they anticipate and pursue circles back to destroy them.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵ֣שׁ מְ֭פַזֵּר וְנוֹסָ֥ף ע֑וֹד וְחוֹשֵׂ֥ךְ מִ֝יֹּ֗שֶׁר אַךְ־לְמַחְסֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: There is that scattereth, and yet increaseth; and there is that withholdeth more than is meet, but it tendeth to poverty.
verses.rendering:
One person gives freely and gains even more;
another withholds what is right and ends up in want.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is one of the most paradoxical proverbs in the collection. The mefazer ('one who scatters, distributes, gives liberally') somehow ve-nosaf od ('and is added to still more') — generosity produces surplus. Meanwhile the chosekh mi-yosher ('one who holds back from what is right, who withholds more than is proper') ends up akh le-machsor ('only in poverty, nothing but want'). The economic logic is inverted: giving produces abundance, hoarding produces scarcity. The sages observed this pattern empirically — the generous person builds social capital, trust, and reciprocity, while the miser isolates himself and forfeits the benefits of community.
translator_notes: The verb mefazer literally means 'to scatter' — the image is of a sower broadcasting seed. Generosity is presented as an investment, not a loss. The word yosher ('uprightness, what is right') in the second line suggests that withholding is not merely stingy but morally wrong.

verses.text_hebrew: נֶֽפֶשׁ־בְּרָכָ֥ה תְדֻשָּׁ֑ן וּ֝מַרְוֶ֗ה גַּם־ה֥וּא יוֹרֶֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: The liberal soul shall be made fat: and he that watereth shall be watered also himself.
verses.rendering:
A generous person will be enriched,
and whoever refreshes others will himself be refreshed.
translator_notes: Nefesh berakhah ('a soul of blessing, a generous soul') is tedusshan ('will be made fat, will be enriched') — in the ancient world, fatness was a sign of prosperity and health, not excess. The marveh ('one who waters, who gives drink, who saturates') gam hu yore ('he also will be watered') — the principle of reciprocity is built into the moral structure of reality.

verses.text_hebrew: מֹ֣נֵֽעַ בָּ֭ר יִקְּבֻ֣הוּ לְא֑וֹם וּ֝בְרָכָ֗ה לְרֹ֣אשׁ מַשְׁבִּֽיר׃
verses.text_kjv: He that withholdeth corn, the people shall curse him: but blessing shall be upon the head of him that selleth it.
verses.rendering:
People curse whoever hoards grain,
but blessing rests on the head of the one who sells it.
translator_notes: The mone'a bar ('withholder of grain') is someone who stockpiles during famine to drive up prices — an ancient form of market manipulation. The community's response is to curse him (yiqqevuhu, 'they will pierce him with curses'). The mashbir ('one who sells, one who distributes grain') receives berakhah — communal blessing and divine favor.

verses.text_hebrew: שֹׁ֣חֵ֣ר ט֭וֹב יְבַקֵּ֣שׁ רָצ֑וֹן וְדֹרֵ֖שׁ רָעָ֣ה תְבוֹאֶֽנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that diligently seeketh good procureth favour: but he that seeketh mischief, it shall come unto him.
verses.rendering:
Whoever earnestly seeks good finds favor,
but whoever pursues evil — it will find him.
translator_notes: The verb shocher ('seeking early, seeking earnestly') implies diligence and urgency — getting up at dawn to pursue something. The ironic reversal in the second line is sharp: the one who seeks evil does not need to find it; it comes to him (tevo'ennu, 'it will come upon him').

verses.text_hebrew: בּוֹטֵ֣חַ בְּ֭עׇשְׁרוֹ ה֣וּא יִפֹּ֑ל וְ֝כֶעָלֶ֗ה צַדִּיקִ֥ים יִפְרָֽחוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He that trusteth in his riches shall fall: but the righteous shall flourish as a branch.
verses.rendering:
Whoever trusts in his wealth will fall,
but the righteous will flourish like a green leaf.
translator_notes: The boreach be-oshro ('one who trusts in his wealth') will yipol ('fall, collapse') — wealth makes a poor foundation. The righteous, by contrast, ke-aleh yifrachu ('like a leaf will flourish, sprout, bud'). The botanical image echoes Psalm 1:3 — the righteous are living things that grow, while the wealth-dependent are structures that collapse.

verses.text_hebrew: עוֹכֵ֣ר בֵּ֭יתוֹ יִנְחַל־ר֑וּחַ וְעֶ֥בֶד אֱ֝וִ֗יל לַחֲכַם־לֵֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: He that troubleth his own house shall inherit the wind: and the fool shall be servant to the wise of heart.
verses.rendering:
Whoever brings trouble on his household will inherit wind,
and a fool will serve the wise in heart.
translator_notes: The oker beto ('troubler of his house') — the one who ruins his own family through greed, folly, or mismanagement — receives the most worthless inheritance imaginable: ruach ('wind'). You cannot hold wind, spend wind, or build on wind. The second line adds that the evil ('fool') ends up as eved ('servant') to the wise — foolishness leads to servitude.

verses.text_hebrew: פְּרִ֣י צַ֭דִּיק עֵ֣ץ חַיִּ֑ים וְלֹקֵ֖חַ נְפָשׁ֣וֹת חָכָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life; and he that winneth souls is wise.
verses.rendering:
The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life,
and the one who captures lives is wise.
verses.expanded_rendering: The peri tsaddiq ('fruit of the righteous') is itself an ets chayyim ('tree of life') — the righteous person's influence and conduct become a source of vitality for others. The ets chayyim echoes Genesis 2-3 and appears four times in Proverbs (3:18, 11:30, 13:12, 15:4), always representing something that sustains and renews life. The second line is debated: loqeach nefashot ('one who takes/captures souls/lives') most likely means the wise person who wins others over, who attracts people to the path of wisdom. Wisdom is magnetic — it draws people toward life.
translator_notes: The 'tree of life' in Proverbs is not identical to the tree in Eden but draws on the same symbolic reservoir — it represents access to enduring, flourishing life. The righteous person becomes this for others.

The phrase loqeach nefashot is ambiguous. Some read it as 'takes lives' (violence) to create an antithetic parallel, but the Masoretic accentuation pairs it with chakham ('wise'), favoring a positive reading: the wise person wins others over.

key_terms.semantic_range: tree of life, source of vitality, sustaining presence
key_terms.note: ets chayyim in Proverbs is a recurring metaphor for whatever gives or sustains life — wisdom (3:18), fulfilled hope (13:12), a healing tongue (15:4), and here the fruit of righteous living. Each use expands the metaphor's range.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵ֣ן צַ֭דִּיק בָּאָ֣רֶץ יְשֻׁלָּ֑ם אַ֝֗ף כִּֽי־רָשָׁ֥ע וְחוֹטֵֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, the righteous shall be recompensed in the earth: much more the wicked and the sinner.
verses.rendering:
If even the righteous receives what he deserves on earth,
how much more the wicked and the sinner!
translator_notes: This closing verse uses a qal va-chomer argument (light to heavy, lesser to greater): if the righteous — who is aligned with God — still faces consequences for his failings in this life, then certainly the wicked and the sinner will face even greater consequences. The verse is a reality check: no one escapes accountability, and the wicked should not imagine that their reckoning is indefinitely postponed.
